deviations.gdpr_quote: (b) the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability;
deviations.policy_quote: However, all requests for rectification must be handled directly by our third-party server host, and you must contact them directly rather than us.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR requires informing about the right to request rectification from the controller; the policy instructs data subjects to contact a third-party host instead.
deviations.analysis: Art 13(2)(b) specifically links the request to the controller; the policy contradicts this by diverting rectification requests away from the controller.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability
deviations.policy_quote: However, all requests for rectification must be handled directly by our third-party server host, and you must contact them directly rather than us.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR specifies rectification requests are to be made to the controller, but the policy directs data subjects to a third-party host instead.
deviations.analysis: Art 13(2)(b) requires informing data subjects that they can request rectification from the controller; the policy reallocates this responsibility to a third party, conflicting with the specified role.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where the controller intends to further process the personal data for a purpose other than that for which the personal data were collected, the controller shall provide the data subject prior to that further processing with information on that other purpose and with any relevant further information as referred to in paragraph 2.
deviations.policy_quote: Your data is not used for any other purposes or shared with third parties.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR allows further processing with prior information, but the policy categorically disallows any other-purpose processing.
deviations.analysis: The policy’s blanket prohibition on further processing is stricter than GDPR Art13(3), which contemplates allowing further processing if prior information (and Art13(2) details) is provided.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where the controller intends to further process the personal data for a purpose other than that for which the personal data were collected, the controller shall provide the data subject prior to that further processing with information on that other purpose and with any relevant further information as referred to in paragraph 2.
deviations.policy_quote: Your data is not used for any other purposes or shared with third parties.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR permits further processing with prior information, whereas the policy categorically forbids any other purposes.
deviations.analysis: By banning further purposes entirely, the policy imposes a stricter constraint than GDPR, which would allow such processing with advance notice including Art 13(2) details.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject and to object to processing as well as the right to data portability
deviations.policy_quote: However, all requests for rectification must be handled directly by our third-party server host, and you must contact them directly rather than us.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR specifies rectification requests are to be made to the controller, but the policy requires data subjects to contact a third-party host instead.
deviations.analysis: Art 14(2)(c) obliges the controller to inform about the right to request rectification from the controller, whereas the policy shifts handling of rectification to a third party, altering who is responsible.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where the controller intends to further process the personal data for a purpose other than that for which the personal data were obtained, the controller shall provide the data subject prior to that further processing with information on that other purpose and with any relevant further information as referred to in paragraph 2.
deviations.policy_quote: Your data is not used for any other purposes or shared with third parties.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR allows further processing with prior information (including Art 14(2) items), while the policy categorically prohibits any other purposes.
deviations.analysis: The policy’s explicit prohibition on further purposes is stricter than GDPR’s conditional allowance to further process with prior disclosure; this is an over-compliance.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed,
deviations.policy_quote: The right of access. However, please note we will not provide confirmation to you as to whether or not personal data concerning you is being processed.
deviations.mismatch: The policy explicitly refuses to provide confirmation of processing, which the GDPR requires as part of the right of access.
deviations.analysis: This is a direct contradiction of the mandatory confirmation element in Art15(1).

deviations.gdpr_quote: (e) the existence of the right to request from the controller rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing of personal data concerning the data subject or to object to such processing;
deviations.policy_quote: However, all requests for rectification must be handled directly by our third-party server host, and you must contact them directly rather than us.
deviations.mismatch: The GDPR specifies the right to request rectification from the controller, while the policy redirects rectification requests to a third-party host.
deviations.analysis: Shifting the rectification request pathway away from the controller conflicts with Art15(1)(e)’s specification that the right is exercisable against the controller.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed
deviations.policy_quote: The right of access. However, please note we will not provide confirmation to you as to whether or not personal data concerning you is being processed.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR grants a right to receive confirmation, while the policy states it will not provide such confirmation.
deviations.analysis: The policy explicitly denies the confirmation aspect of access that Art 15(1) guarantees.

deviations.gdpr_quote: access to the personal data and the following information:
deviations.policy_quote: if you have left comments on the Website, you can request to receive an exported file of the personal data we hold about you.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR provides access to personal data generally, but the policy limits access to users who have left comments.
deviations.analysis: By conditioning access on having left comments, the policy narrows the scope of data subjects and data covered under the access right.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the existence of the right to request from the controller rectification or erasure of personal data
deviations.policy_quote: However, all requests for rectification must be handled directly by our third-party server host, and you must contact them directly rather than us.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR frames rectification requests as being made to the controller, whereas the policy diverts rectification to a third-party host.
deviations.analysis: Art 15(1)(e) confirms the right to request rectification from the controller; delegating this to a third party shifts responsibility away from the controller.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed
deviations.policy_quote: However, please note we will not provide confirmation to you as to whether or not personal data concerning you is being processed.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR guarantees a right to receive confirmation of processing, whereas the policy states it will not provide such confirmation.
deviations.analysis: This directly negates the confirmation element of the access right under Art 15(1). While Art 15(1)(h) references Art 22(1), the policy is silent on automated decisions; per instructions, omissions are treated as Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies
deviations.policy_quote: It is removed upon your request.
deviations.mismatch: The policy promises unconditional erasure on request (for legitimate-interest data), whereas GDPR makes erasure conditional on specified grounds (and in legitimate-interest contexts can be refused if overriding grounds exist).
deviations.analysis: Art17(1) conditions erasure on one of several grounds; for legitimate-interest processing, Art21(1) (cross-referenced by Art17(1)(c)) allows refusal where compelling overriding grounds exist. The policy’s unconditional deletion on request for legitimate-interest data is stricter than the GDPR requires.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6(1), or point (a) of Article 9(2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing
deviations.policy_quote: It is removed upon your withdrawal of consent or your request to terminate these services.
deviations.mismatch: The policy commits to erase data upon consent withdrawal regardless of whether another legal ground exists, while GDPR requires erasure only if no other legal ground remains.
deviations.analysis: GDPR allows continued processing on another lawful basis after consent withdrawal; the policy’s unconditional erasure is stricter than required.

deviations.gdpr_quote: in a structured, commonly used and machine-readable format
deviations.policy_quote: Furthermore, data portability requests will be fulfilled exclusively by printing your data on physical paper and mailing it to your address.
deviations.mismatch: The policy narrows portability to paper-only, whereas the GDPR requires a structured, commonly used, machine-readable format.
deviations.analysis: Limiting portability responses to printed paper alters the required data format, removing the machine-readable element mandated by the GDPR.

deviations.gdpr_quote: and have the right to transmit those data to another controller without hindrance from the controller to which the personal data have been provided
deviations.policy_quote: Furthermore, data portability requests will be fulfilled exclusively by printing your data on physical paper and mailing it to your address.
deviations.mismatch: The policy’s paper-by-mail approach to the data subject hinders direct transmission to another controller without hindrance.
deviations.analysis: By only mailing paper copies to the data subject, the controller’s method impedes frictionless transfer to another controller, deviating from the GDPR’s prescribed modality of enabling unhindered transmission.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing.
deviations.policy_quote: The right to object is strictly limited to our use of your email for direct marketing campaigns.
deviations.mismatch: The policy narrows the right to object only to email-based direct marketing, whereas the GDPR grants a right to object to any processing for direct marketing purposes, including related profiling.
deviations.analysis: By restricting objections to email campaigns, the policy excludes other direct marketing channels and related profiling, narrowing the GDPR’s scope.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing.
deviations.policy_quote: The right to object. The right to object is strictly limited to our use of your email for direct marketing campaigns.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR provides a right to object to any processing for direct marketing (including profiling), but the policy narrows it to email direct marketing only.
deviations.analysis: This limits the scope of the right to object, excluding non-email direct marketing and profiling related to direct marketing that Art 21(2) covers.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where personal data are processed for direct marketing purposes, the data subject shall have the right to object at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her for such marketing, which includes profiling to the extent that it is related to such direct marketing.
deviations.policy_quote: The right to object is strictly limited to our use of your email for direct marketing campaigns.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR grants the right to object to any processing for direct marketing (including related profiling), whereas the policy restricts it to email-based direct marketing only.
deviations.analysis: The policy narrows the scope from all processing for direct marketing to just email use, excluding other channels and related profiling.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where the data subject objects to processing for direct marketing purposes, the personal data shall no longer be processed for such purposes.
deviations.policy_quote: The right to object. The right to object is strictly limited to our use of your email for direct marketing campaigns.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR requires all processing for direct marketing purposes to cease upon objection, while the policy restricts objections (and thus cessation) to email direct marketing only.
deviations.analysis: By limiting objections to email, the policy effectively narrows the set of processing that would stop after an objection, excluding other direct marketing channels that Art 21(3) would cover.

deviations.gdpr_quote: Where personal data are processed for scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes pursuant to Article 89(1), the data subject, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, shall have the right to object to processing of personal data concerning him or her, unless the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for reasons of public interest.
deviations.policy_quote: You may opt out of this tracking at any time by activating the “Do Not Track” setting in your browser.
deviations.mismatch: The GDPR grants a conditional right to object based on the data subject’s particular situation, whereas the policy provides an unconditional, anytime opt-out for the statistical tracking.
deviations.analysis: Art21(6) conditions the right to object on the data subject’s particular situation in the context of Art89(1); the policy offers an unconditional opt-out via DNT, which is stricter (more permissive for data subjects) than required.

deviations.gdpr_quote: In the case of a personal data breach, the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55, unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
deviations.policy_quote: We will ensure the supervisory authority is notified within 12 hours of our team becoming aware of the breach.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR allows up to 72 hours where feasible, but the policy imposes a 12-hour deadline.
deviations.analysis: The policy’s 12-hour commitment is stricter than the GDPR’s ‘without undue delay’ and ‘not later than 72 hours where feasible’ standard.

deviations.gdpr_quote: …unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
deviations.policy_quote: In the event of a data breach, we will first issue a press release to the public media, and subsequently notify the supervisory authority.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR allows not notifying the supervisory authority if the breach is unlikely to pose a risk, whereas the policy commits to notifying the authority in every breach event.
deviations.analysis: By committing to notify regardless of risk, the policy removes GDPR’s conditional exception, creating an over-compliant requirement.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the controller shall without undue delay and, where feasible, not later than 72 hours after having become aware of it, notify the personal data breach to the supervisory authority competent in accordance with Article 55
deviations.policy_quote: We will ensure the supervisory authority is notified within 12 hours of our team becoming aware of the breach.
deviations.mismatch: The policy imposes a 12-hour notification deadline, which is stricter than the GDPR’s “where feasible, not later than 72 hours” requirement.
deviations.analysis: This is an over-compliant internal SLA (stricter timing than Art33(1)). Art33(1) also requires notifying the SA competent per Art55 (REG-087 to REG-089); the policy does not specify competence, but that omission is not treated as a deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: unless the personal data breach is unlikely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons.
deviations.policy_quote: In the event of a data breach, we will first issue a press release to the public media, and subsequently notify the supervisory authority.
deviations.mismatch: The policy commits to notifying the supervisory authority for any data breach, whereas the GDPR does not require notification if the breach is unlikely to result in risk.
deviations.analysis: By omitting the GDPR’s risk-based exemption, the policy is stricter than Art33(1). Cross-reference to Art55 competence (REG-087 to REG-089) is acknowledged; it does not affect this over-compliance assessment.
